shared.alpha_banner: kcore is alpha software under active development. Not production ready.

footer.description: A modern virtualization alternative. API-first, declarative, built for operators and automation.

hero.subtitle: kcore is an API-first virtualization platform built for operators who want a cleaner, more programmable way to manage VMs. Declarative configuration, automation-friendly design, and a modern control plane.
hero.alpha_warning: Alpha software. Not production ready. For early testers, labs, and design partners.

why.title: Why kcore
why.subtitle: Virtualization platforms haven't kept up with how operators actually work
why.api_first_desc: Every operation is available through a gRPC API. No more clicking through web UIs to do what a script should handle.
why.declarative_desc: Define your infrastructure as code. Version control your VMs, networks, and storage. Reproduce environments reliably.
why.automation_desc: Designed from the ground up so tools, scripts, and agents can manage infrastructure without human intervention.
why.immutable_desc: The host OS is immutable. Updates are atomic with instant rollback. No more broken nodes after a bad upgrade.
why.modern_desc: Go control plane, gRPC APIs, NixOS-based host. No legacy PHP, no Perl scripts, no decades of accumulated cruft.
why.agent_desc: AI agents are first-class citizens. The platform is designed so automation agents can interact with it naturally through APIs and declarative workflows.

different.title: What Makes kcore Different
different.subtitle: Proxmox works. But it was designed in a different era. kcore starts fresh with the assumption that infrastructure should be programmable, reproducible, and managed by software — not just by humans clicking through a web UI.
different.api_first_desc: The API is the primary interface. The UI is a consumer of the API, not the other way around.
different.declarative_desc: Describe what you want. kcore converges to that state. No imperative step-by-step provisioning.
different.agents_desc: The platform is designed so AI agents and automation tools can control it through the same APIs humans use. Machine-friendly by default.
different.clean_desc: Written in Go. No legacy dependencies. No accumulated technical debt from two decades of patches.

status.title: Current Status
status.subtitle: Honest about where we are
status.maturity_desc: kcore is under active development. Core functionality is being built and tested. APIs and interfaces may change without notice.

who.title: Who kcore Is For
who.subtitle: Built for people who think about infrastructure differently
who.homelab_desc: You run your own hardware and want something cleaner than Proxmox without the complexity of OpenStack. You value simplicity and control.
who.hosting_desc: You manage a handful of servers for clients or at edge locations. You need something lightweight, scriptable, and easy to reason about.
who.engineers_desc: You want to manage VMs the same way you manage everything else — through APIs, version-controlled config, and automation pipelines.
who.early_desc: You're comfortable running alpha software. You want to shape the direction of a new virtualization platform and give feedback that matters.

code.title: Declarative by Default
code.subtitle: Define your infrastructure with clean, readable code

cta.title: Interested in Testing kcore?
cta.desc: kcore is alpha software. We're looking for early testers, design partners, and operators who want to help shape a modern virtualization platform.
cta.note: Suitable for experimentation, home labs, and early design partners only. Not for production use.

features.hero_subtitle: How kcore approaches virtualization differently. API-first, declarative, automation-friendly.
features.p_api_desc: The gRPC API is the primary interface. Every operation — creating VMs, managing storage, configuring networks — goes through the API. The CLI, web UI, and any agent tooling are all API consumers.
features.p_declarative_desc: You describe the desired state. kcore converges to it. No step-by-step scripts that break when something changes. State reconciliation is built into the core loop.
features.p_immutable_desc: The base system does not change at runtime. Updates are applied as new system generations. If an update fails, you boot the previous generation. No partial states.
features.p_agents_desc: AI agents and automation tools interact with kcore through the same APIs that humans use. The platform is machine-friendly by design — structured inputs, predictable outputs, no GUI-only workflows.
features.p_simple_desc: kcore favors straightforward solutions. A small, correct system is better than a large, feature-complete one that's hard to reason about. Complexity is the enemy.
features.p_operator_desc: You own your hardware, your data, and your configuration. kcore does not phone home, require a cloud account, or impose licensing restrictions on how you use it.
features.atomic_desc: The host OS is built on NixOS. System updates produce a new generation. If something goes wrong, you boot the previous generation. There is no "partially applied update" state.
features.atomic_immutable_desc: The core OS is defined declaratively. Runtime changes don't persist.
features.atomic_rollback_desc: Every update creates a new bootable generation. Rollback is a reboot, not a repair.
features.atomic_reproducible_desc: Same inputs produce the same system image. No configuration drift across nodes.
features.declarative_desc: Define what you want. kcore makes it happen. Version control your entire infrastructure. Reproduce it on another set of hardware with the same config files.
features.declarative_terraform_desc: Manage kcore resources alongside your existing Terraform workflows. (In development.)
features.declarative_yaml_desc: Define VMs, networks, and storage in plain YAML. Apply with kctl.
features.declarative_vcs_desc: All configuration is text. Commit it to git. Review changes in PRs. Deploy from CI.
features.agent_desc: kcore is designed so that automation agents — including AI agents — can interact with the platform through the same interfaces humans use. No special "AI mode." The platform is machine-friendly by default.
features.agent_mcp_desc: Built-in Model Context Protocol server lets AI agents query and manage infrastructure. (In development.)
features.agent_api_desc: gRPC with typed schemas. Agents get predictable inputs and outputs, not scraped HTML or CLI parsing.
features.agent_workflows_desc: Agents describe desired state. The platform handles convergence. No multi-step imperative sequences to get wrong.
features.compare_subtitle: An honest look at the current state. kcore is alpha software.
features.compare_note: kcore is alpha software and does not yet match Proxmox in feature completeness or stability. This table reflects architectural direction, not current parity.
features.cta_desc: We're looking for early testers and design partners who want to help build a modern alternative to Proxmox.
features.cta_note: Alpha software. For labs, experimentation, and early design partners only.

roadmap.hero_subtitle: Where kcore is today and where it's headed. Updated as development progresses.
roadmap.intro_p1: kcore is alpha software. The roadmap below reflects current development priorities but is subject to change. We ship when things are ready, not to hit artificial dates.
roadmap.intro_p2: Milestones are grouped into phases. Each phase builds on the previous one. We won't skip ahead — foundations matter.
roadmap.phase1_desc: Core platform architecture, basic VM lifecycle, CLI tooling.
roadmap.phase2_desc: Terraform provider, MCP server, and the tooling that makes kcore programmable.
roadmap.phase3_desc: Web dashboard, HA, monitoring, and the features needed for serious use.
roadmap.m_nixos_desc: Declarative host configuration with atomic updates and generation-based rollback.
roadmap.m_vm_desc: Create, start, stop, delete VMs from the command line. Basic CRUD operations.
roadmap.m_grpc_desc: Typed API for programmatic access. All CLI operations available via API.
roadmap.m_multinode_desc: Controller + node-agent topology. Nodes self-register with the controller on boot.
roadmap.m_storage_desc: Store VM disks on local directories or LVM volumes.
roadmap.m_networking_desc: Automated bridge setup, VLAN support, network configuration for VMs.
roadmap.m_images_desc: Download, cache, and manage OS images. Support for cloud-init enabled images.
roadmap.m_mtls_desc: Encrypted, authenticated communication across the cluster.
roadmap.m_yaml_desc: Declarative VM definitions applied from files, similar to kubectl apply.
roadmap.m_terraform_desc: Manage kcore VMs, networks, and storage through Terraform. Declarative infrastructure as code.
roadmap.m_mcp_desc: Model Context Protocol server so AI agents can query and manage infrastructure through structured APIs.
roadmap.m_zfs_desc: Snapshots, clones, and compression for VM storage using ZFS.
roadmap.m_snapshots_desc: Create point-in-time snapshots and clone VMs for testing and development.
roadmap.m_scheduling_desc: Automatic placement of VMs across nodes based on available resources.
roadmap.m_dashboard_desc: Read-only dashboard for cluster visibility. API consumer, not a primary interface.
roadmap.m_ha_desc: Automatic VM restart on node failure. Controller redundancy.
roadmap.m_migration_desc: Move running VMs between nodes without downtime.
roadmap.m_monitoring_desc: Built-in metrics, health checks, and integration with Prometheus/Grafana.
roadmap.m_backup_desc: Scheduled backups with configurable retention policies.
roadmap.cta_desc: We're building kcore with input from early testers and design partners. If you have use cases that should shape our priorities, we want to hear from you.

architecture.hero_subtitle: How kcore is built. Go control plane, NixOS host, gRPC APIs, multi-node topology.
architecture.overview_p1: kcore is a virtualization platform with a distributed architecture. A central controller manages cluster state while node agents run on each physical machine, managing VMs through libvirt. All communication is via gRPC with mTLS.
architecture.overview_p2: The host OS is NixOS — fully declarative and immutable at runtime. The control plane is written in Go. There is no PHP, no Perl, no legacy web framework. The API is the product.
architecture.control_desc: The controller is a single Go binary that manages cluster state. It exposes a gRPC API that the CLI, Terraform provider, MCP server, and any other tooling consumes.
architecture.nixos_desc: Each compute node runs NixOS. The entire system configuration is defined in Nix — packages, services, network, storage. The system is immutable at runtime. Changes require a rebuild that produces a new bootable generation.
architecture.grpc_desc: The API is the primary interface to kcore. Everything the CLI can do, the API can do. Everything a Terraform provider needs, the API provides. AI agents interact through the same API — no special endpoints, no separate "agent mode."
architecture.storage_subtitle: Use the storage backend that fits your hardware and use case.
architecture.storage_local_desc: Store VM disk images as files on a local filesystem. Simple, fast, no dependencies. Good for single-node setups and home labs.
architecture.storage_lvm_desc: Logical Volume Manager for flexible disk allocation. Thin provisioning, snapshots. Recommended for multi-VM hosts.
architecture.storage_zfs_desc: Copy-on-write filesystem with built-in snapshots, compression, and checksumming. Ideal for data integrity and advanced storage needs.
architecture.consumers_subtitle: The same API serves every interface. No special cases.
architecture.consumer_kctl_desc: Primary operator tool. Create, manage, and inspect VMs and nodes. Thin wrapper around the gRPC API.
architecture.consumer_terraform_desc: Manage kcore resources as infrastructure as code. Integrates with existing Terraform workflows.
architecture.consumer_mcp_desc: Model Context Protocol server for AI agent integration. Agents query and manage infrastructure through structured tools.
architecture.consumer_dashboard_desc: Read-only cluster visibility. The dashboard consumes the API — it does not have special access.
architecture.cta_desc: Read the documentation for setup guides, API reference, and CLI usage. Or reach out if you want to discuss the architecture.

faq.hero_subtitle: Honest answers about kcore — what it is, what it isn't, and where it's going.
faq.a_what: kcore is a virtualization platform — a modern alternative to Proxmox. It lets you manage VMs on bare metal servers using an API-first, declarative approach. The host OS is NixOS (immutable), the control plane is written in Go, and the API is gRPC.
faq.a_production: No. kcore is alpha software. It is under active development and APIs may change without notice. It is suitable for experimentation, home labs, and early design partners. Do not use it for production workloads or business-critical infrastructure.
faq.q_compare: How does kcore compare to Proxmox?
faq.a_compare_p1: Proxmox is a mature, battle-tested platform with a large community. kcore is not trying to replicate Proxmox feature-for-feature. Instead, it takes a different architectural approach: API-first (not UI-first), declarative configuration, immutable host OS, and first-class support for automation agents.
faq.a_compare_p2: Proxmox is the right choice if you need production stability today. kcore is for operators who want a cleaner, more programmable foundation and are willing to test alpha software.
faq.a_who_builds: kcore is built by Tacconi Consulting Ltd, a platform engineering consultancy based in England. We build and operate infrastructure for a living — kcore comes from real experience with the limitations of existing virtualization platforms.
faq.a_alpha_means: Alpha means: core functionality works but is incomplete. APIs and configuration formats may change between releases. There is no stability guarantee. Things may break. We fix things quickly, but you should expect rough edges.
faq.a_when_beta: We don't set arbitrary deadlines. Beta means: the Terraform provider works, networking is solid, the API is stable enough to build on, and there's a web dashboard for visibility. Check the roadmap for current progress.
faq.q_homelab: Can I use kcore in my home lab right now?
faq.a_homelab_p1: Yes, with caveats. If you are comfortable with alpha software, know your way around NixOS, and don't mind things changing — kcore works for home lab experimentation. Build the ISO, boot from USB, install to disk, and start creating VMs.
faq.a_homelab_p2: If you need something stable for important workloads, use Proxmox for now and keep an eye on kcore.
faq.q_migrate: Can I migrate from Proxmox to kcore?
faq.a_migrate: Not yet. There is no automated migration path from Proxmox to kcore. kcore is alpha software and not a drop-in replacement. When kcore reaches a more mature state, we plan to document migration strategies for common Proxmox setups.
faq.q_alongside: Can I run kcore alongside Proxmox?
faq.a_alongside: Yes. kcore installs on separate hardware. You can run a kcore node on a spare machine or dedicated test server while keeping your Proxmox cluster running. They don't interact with each other.
faq.a_vmware: If you're looking for a VMware exit path today, Proxmox is the pragmatic choice — it's mature and production-ready. kcore is a longer-term option for operators who want a more modern, programmable foundation. We'd rather you use the right tool now than adopt something that isn't ready.
faq.a_hardware: Any x86_64 machine with hardware virtualization support (Intel VT-x or AMD-V). At minimum: 4GB RAM, 1 NIC, 1 disk. For useful VM workloads, 16GB+ RAM and SSD storage is recommended.
faq.q_why_nixos: Why NixOS instead of Debian or Ubuntu?
faq.a_why_nixos_p1: NixOS gives us properties that matter for a hypervisor host: immutable system state, atomic updates, instant rollback, and reproducible builds. Every node built from the same flake is identical. There is no configuration drift. A bad update is a reboot away from being undone.
faq.a_why_nixos_p2: Debian and Ubuntu are mutable — packages update in place, config files drift, and rollback is manual. Proxmox inherits these properties from Debian. We chose differently.
faq.a_why_grpc_p1: gRPC with Protocol Buffers gives us typed schemas, code generation, streaming, and efficient binary serialization. It's better for machine-to-machine communication — which is what matters when your API is the primary interface, not a web UI backend.
faq.a_why_grpc_p2: Clients can be generated in Go, Python, Rust, TypeScript, and other languages directly from the proto definitions. Agents get structured data, not JSON blobs to parse.
faq.q_ai_agents: What does "AI agents are first-class citizens" mean?
faq.a_ai_agents_p1: It means the platform is designed so automation agents — including AI agents — can interact with it through the same APIs humans use. There is no GUI-only workflow. The API has structured, typed inputs and outputs. The MCP server (in development) lets AI agents discover and use kcore operations through a standard protocol.
faq.a_ai_agents_p2: This is not "AI-powered infrastructure" marketing. It's a design principle: if software can't control it programmatically, it's a usability bug.
faq.q_license: What license is kcore released under?
faq.a_license: Licensing details will be published as the project approaches beta. The intent is to keep kcore usable for individuals, home labs, and small operators without restrictive licensing. Contact us at team@tacconiconsulting.com if you have specific licensing questions.
faq.q_paid: Will there be a paid version?
faq.a_paid: The business model is not finalised. If there is a paid tier, it will be for commercial support, enterprise features, or managed services — not for gating core functionality. We'll be transparent about this as the project matures.
faq.cta_title: Have a Question We Didn't Cover?
faq.cta_desc: Reach out. We're happy to answer questions about kcore, its architecture, or where it's headed.

blog.hero_subtitle: Development updates, technical decisions, and progress reports.
blog.post4_title: Why I Built kcore: Proxmox Broke My Automation One Too Many Times
blog.post4_p1: I've been running Proxmox for years. On paper, it's great: open source, KVM-based, web UI included. In practice, every time I tried to automate anything beyond the basics, I hit a wall.
blog.post4_h_api: The API That Isn't Really an API
blog.post4_p2: Proxmox has a REST API. Technically. But it's a thin wrapper over the web UI, not a first-class interface. Try doing something simple like attaching a cloud-init drive or configuring a network bridge programmatically — you'll end up shelling out to qm or pvesh from your Terraform or Ansible playbook. The Terraform provider for Proxmox is a community project that constantly fights against API limitations. Half the time you're writing provisioner "remote-exec" blocks to run CLI commands on the host because the API simply doesn't expose what you need.
blog.post4_p3: That's not automation. That's scripting around an incomplete interface.
blog.post4_h_legacy: Perl, Corosync, and Legacy All the Way Down
blog.post4_p4: Look under Proxmox's hood and you'll find Perl — lots of it. The core management framework, pve-manager, is written in Perl. The API server is Perl. The cluster stack depends on Corosync and pmxcfs, a custom clustered filesystem. It's engineering from a different era. Nobody is excited about debugging Perl stack traces in 2026. When something goes wrong — and in clustering, things go wrong — you're reading through code that feels like it belongs in a museum.
blog.post4_p5: Ask anyone running Proxmox in a serious deployment what version they're on. It's almost never the latest. Upgrading Proxmox is a manual, nerve-wracking process. You're apt upgrade-ing a mutable Debian system that's running your production VMs. There's no atomic rollback. If something breaks mid-upgrade — a kernel incompatibility, a Corosync version mismatch, a ZFS module that didn't rebuild — you're in for a long night. So people don't upgrade. They run Proxmox 7 when 8 has been out for years. They skip point releases. They treat their hypervisor like a legacy appliance: don't touch it, pray it keeps working.
blog.post4_p6: This is the opposite of how modern infrastructure should work.
blog.post4_p7: kcore runs on NixOS. The entire host OS is defined declaratively in a single configuration. Every node built from the same flake is identical — no drift, no snowflakes, no "works on node 3 but not node 7." Updates produce a new system generation. If the update breaks something, you reboot into the previous generation. Done. No partial states, no manual rollback procedures, no downtime prayer sessions.
blog.post4_p8: Updating a 10-node kcore cluster means pushing a new flake and rebuilding. Every node converges to the same state. If one node has a problem, it rolls back independently. Compare that to upgrading 10 Proxmox nodes one by one, crossing your fingers each time.
blog.post4_p9: kcore is what I wanted Proxmox to be. The API is the product — every operation goes through gRPC with typed schemas. The CLI is a thin client. The Terraform provider talks to the same API. There's no web UI you have to click through to complete a workflow that the API forgot.
blog.post4_p10: VMs are defined in YAML and applied declaratively. The control plane is written in Go — not Perl, not PHP, not a 20-year-old web framework. The codebase is small, readable, and modern.
blog.post4_p11: Is it done? No. kcore is alpha software. It doesn't have HA clustering, live migration, or a web dashboard yet. Proxmox does, and if you need those things today, use Proxmox. I'm not pretending otherwise.
blog.post4_p12: But if you've ever stared at a Proxmox Terraform run that failed because the API doesn't support what the UI does, or held your breath through a cluster upgrade, or wondered why your hypervisor's core is written in Perl — kcore is being built for you.
blog.post4_p13: It's early. The foundation is solid. And I'd rather build the right thing slowly than ship another legacy platform. I'd love to hear from you if this resonates.
blog.post3_p1: We've relaunched the kcore website with clearer, more honest positioning. The previous version leaned too heavily on AI marketing language and implied a maturity level we haven't reached. That's been corrected.
blog.post3_p2: kcore is now positioned as what it is: a modern Proxmox alternative in alpha. The messaging is straightforward — API-first, declarative, built for automation, not production ready. AI agents are first-class citizens in the platform design, but kcore is not an "AI platform."
blog.post3_p3: New pages include this blog, a detailed roadmap, a technical architecture overview, and a FAQ that addresses maturity, Proxmox migration, and licensing honestly.
blog.post2_p1: The controller + node-agent architecture is functional. Nodes self-register with the controller on boot and report their capabilities (CPU, memory, storage). The controller tracks cluster state in SQLite and routes VM operations to the appropriate node agent via gRPC.
blog.post2_p2: Current topology: one controller (runs on your workstation or a management node) and N compute nodes. Each compute node runs NixOS with libvirtd and the kcore node-agent service.
blog.post2_next_1: mTLS between controller and agents (currently using insecure gRPC for development)
blog.post2_next_2: Automatic certificate provisioning on node registration
blog.post2_next_3: Node health checks and heartbeat monitoring
blog.post1_p1: We've been running Proxmox in production for years. It works. But every time we needed to automate something — provision a VM from CI, template a cluster, reproduce an environment — we hit the same friction. The API is an afterthought to the web UI. The configuration model is imperative. The host OS is mutable Debian with all the drift that implies.
blog.post1_p2: We wanted a virtualization platform that:
blog.post1_li1: Has an API as its primary interface, not a web UI
blog.post1_li2: Uses declarative configuration — describe what you want, not the steps to get there
blog.post1_li3: Runs on an immutable host OS with atomic updates and instant rollback
blog.post1_li4: Is built for automation from day one — scripts, Terraform, AI agents, whatever tools you use
blog.post1_p3: Nothing like this existed, so we started building it. kcore is the result. It's very early — alpha, not production ready — but the foundation is solid: Go control plane, NixOS hosts, gRPC APIs, and a clean codebase with no legacy baggage.
blog.post1_p4: If you're an operator who thinks about infrastructure this way, we'd like to hear from you.
blog.cta_desc: We post development updates here as we ship. For questions or early access, reach out directly.

qs.title: Quick Start
qs.intro: From bare metal to running VMs — using kctl for the full lifecycle.
qs.note_alpha: kcore is alpha software. This guide assumes you have a dedicated machine or lab environment for testing.
qs.prereq1: Nix with flakes enabled (for building the ISO)
qs.prereq5: A dedicated x86_64 machine with KVM support (Intel VT-x or AMD-V)
qs.build_iso_includes: The ISO includes the kcore node-agent, libvirtd, virtlogd, the Automator API, and all dependencies.
qs.write_usb_desc: Boot from USB and log in with root / kcore. The network auto-configures via DHCP.
qs.init_cluster_desc: On your workstation, create a cluster identity and CA:
qs.init_cluster_note: This generates a cluster CA under ~/.kcore/my-cluster/. The CA key stays on your machine.
qs.discover_desc: Query the node for available disks and NICs:
qs.install_node_desc: Trigger OS installation with multi-disk support:
qs.install_node_reboot: After reboot, the node auto-registers with the controller via mTLS.
qs.network_declarative: Or declaratively with kctl apply -f node-network.yaml (kind: NodeNetwork).
qs.create_vm_desc: VMs are created declaratively via YAML manifests:
qs.terraform_desc: You can also manage VMs with the kcore Terraform provider:

arch.title: Architecture
arch.intro: kcore is a clustered KVM virtualization platform built with Go, gRPC, and NixOS. This page describes the system topology, component responsibilities, and data flows.
arch.controller_first_p1: All external clients — kctl and the Terraform provider — communicate exclusively with the controller on port 9090. Only the controller talks to node-agents on port 9091. This is a hard rule: kctl and Terraform never create direct connections to node-agents.
arch.controller_first_p2: This single-entry-point design simplifies security (one TLS endpoint to expose), avoids split-brain coordination issues, and keeps node-agents invisible to end users.
arch.desired_actual_p1: When a user submits a YAML manifest or Terraform resource, the controller stores it as desired state in its SQLite database. The controller never modifies desired state autonomously — it is the user's source of truth.
arch.desired_actual_li1: Desired state — the full resource spec including target lifecycle state (running, stopped, or deleted). Only changed by explicit user action.
arch.desired_actual_li2: Actual state — reported by node-agents: VM power state, actual node placement, resource usage. Updated via heartbeats and sync RPCs.
arch.desired_actual_p2: A reconciliation loop continuously compares desired to actual for every VM and takes action to converge — creating, starting, stopping, or deleting VMs as needed.
arch.table_controller_role: Cluster coordinator. Stores desired/actual state in SQLite, serves gRPC API, drives reconciliation.
arch.table_nodeagent_role: Per-node daemon. Manages local VMs via libvirt, reports state to controller, handles storage and networking.
arch.table_kctl_role: CLI client. Sends gRPC requests to the controller. Supports declarative manifests (kctl apply -f) and imperative queries (kctl get vms).
arch.table_terraform_role: Terraform provider. Manages VMs as HCL resources via the same controller gRPC API.
arch.automator_p1: The Automator gRPC service (replacing the older NodeAdmin service) covers the full bare-metal node lifecycle. It is exposed on the same node-agent gRPC port (9091).
arch.automator_li1: Hardware discovery — GetDisks, GetNics: enumerate local block devices and network interfaces.
arch.automator_li2: OS installation — InstallNode: multi-disk, role-based NixOS installation with automatic partitioning.
arch.automator_li3: Network configuration — ConfigureNetwork: bridges, bonds (LACP, active-backup), VLANs, DNS.
arch.automator_li4: NixOS config push — UpdateNixConfig: deploy a new NixOS configuration to a node.
arch.automator_li5: System update — UpdateSystem: trigger a nixos-rebuild switch on the node.
arch.bootstrap_p1: When a bare-metal node first boots from the kcore ISO, no TLS infrastructure exists yet. The bootstrap sequence establishes trust and configures the node:
arch.bootstrap_li1: Node boots from the kcore ISO (minimal NixOS with node-agent in insecure mode).
arch.bootstrap_li2: kctl init cluster generates the cluster CA and controller certificates on the operator's machine.
arch.bootstrap_li3: kctl get disks and kctl get nics discover the node's hardware via the Automator API.
arch.bootstrap_li4: kctl install node triggers NixOS installation on selected disks and issues TLS certificates for the node.
arch.bootstrap_li5: After reboot, the node-agent starts with TLS enabled and registers with the controller.
arch.ca_p1: The cluster CA lives on the operator's machine at ~/.kcore/<cluster>/. The CA private key never leaves the operator's machine — it is not copied to nodes or the controller.
arch.ca_p2: Node and controller certificates are issued during the installation process. Each component receives its own certificate signed by the cluster CA, enabling mutual TLS authentication across the cluster.
arch.multidisk_p1: The InstallNode RPC supports multiple disks, each assigned a role:
arch.multidisk_li1: os — root filesystem. Exactly one disk must have this role. Partitioned with boot, swap, and root partitions.
arch.multidisk_li2: storage — LVM thin-pool for VM disks. Zero or more disks. Initialized as LVM physical volumes and added to a volume group for thin-provisioned VM storage.
arch.network_p1: The ConfigureNetwork RPC supports complex networking topologies rendered into NixOS networking modules:
arch.network_li1: Bridges — for VM connectivity, attaching VMs to physical networks.
arch.network_li2: Bonds — LACP (802.3ad) and active-backup modes for link aggregation and redundancy.
arch.network_p2: Configuration can be applied immediately using the applyNow flag, which triggers a NixOS rebuild after writing the network config.
arch.grpc_ctrl_li1: RegisterNode, Heartbeat — node registration and health monitoring
arch.grpc_ctrl_li2: SyncVmState — node-agents report VM state changes
arch.grpc_ctrl_li3: VM lifecycle — CreateVm, StartVm, StopVm, DeleteVm, ListVms, GetVm
arch.grpc_node_li1: NodeCompute — VM operations (create, start, stop, delete domains via libvirt)
arch.grpc_node_li2: NodeStorage — disk image management, LVM thin-pool operations

cli.title: CLI Reference
cli.intro: kctl is the command-line interface for managing kcore clusters. kctl communicates with the controller (port 9090), which forwards operations to node-agents.
cli.init_cluster_desc: Generates a cluster CA and identity. The CA key stays on the operator machine.
cli.discover_hw_desc: Query the node-agent for block devices and NICs before installation.
cli.update_system_flags: Flags: --channels (update NixOS channels), --rebuild (nixos-rebuild switch), --agent (upgrade node-agent binary).
cli.create_desc: The create command is used for volumes and networks, not VMs. Use kctl apply -f for VMs.
cli.provisioning_manifests_intro: In addition to VM manifests, kctl supports three provisioning manifest kinds:
cli.manifest_nodeinstall: NodeInstall — multi-disk OS installation with controller role option
cli.manifest_nodeconfig: NodeConfig — NixOS configuration push with rebuild option
cli.config_desc: kctl reads its config from ~/.kcore/config by default. The config supports multiple contexts:
cli.config_override: You can override the config with flags:
